title: Why the James Webb Space Telescope Is Changing Everything We Thought We Knew
body:
The James Webb Space Telescope, launched on Christmas Day 2021 after decades of development and repeated delays, has exceeded even the optimistic expectations of its creators. In its first years of operation, it has produced images of unprecedented clarity and depth, detected the atmospheric signatures of exoplanets, observed the earliest galaxies in the universe, and challenged several established models of cosmic structure and evolution.

The telescope's primary advantage over its predecessor, the Hubble, is its sensitivity to infrared light — wavelengths that carry light from the earliest galaxies, which has been redshifted by the expansion of the universe to frequencies invisible to optical telescopes. By observing in infrared, Webb can see further back in time than any previous instrument — to within a few hundred million years of the Big Bang.

Among the most significant early findings: galaxies in the very early universe appear to be larger and more structured than theoretical models predicted. The standard model of cosmological evolution suggested that the earliest galaxies should be small, irregular, and relatively simple — only gradually growing into the massive, complex structures observed in the more recent universe. Webb has found large, well-organised galaxies at epochs where, according to existing theory, they should not yet exist. This does not invalidate the Big Bang model, but it suggests that the early universe was significantly more complex than we thought.

Webb has also made the first direct detection of carbon dioxide in an exoplanet atmosphere, and continued its predecessors' work identifying the chemical signatures of potentially habitable worlds. The search for biosignatures — chemical evidence of biological processes — is now a realistic observational programme rather than a theoretical aspiration.

title: CRISPR and the Future of Human Genetics
body:
CRISPR-Cas9, the gene editing technology adapted from a bacterial immune system by Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier (who shared the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the work), has transformed the landscape of genetic science since its first application in human cells in 2013. It is not the first gene editing technology, but it is the first that is cheap, precise, and accessible enough to be used routinely in laboratories worldwide.

The mechanism is elegant: Cas9 is a protein that acts as molecular scissors, capable of cutting DNA at a specific location; guide RNA directs it to the exact sequence to be cut. Once the DNA is cut, the cell's own repair machinery can be directed to delete the cut sequence, correct it, or insert a new sequence in its place. The precision is extraordinary by previous standards, though off-target effects remain an area of active research.

Current medical applications include treatments for sickle cell disease (the first CRISPR-based therapy approved by the FDA, in late 2023), several cancers, and certain inherited blindness disorders. These are primarily ex vivo treatments — cells are removed from the patient, edited in a laboratory, and returned — which avoids some of the complications of delivering CRISPR directly to the body.

The more speculative frontier is germline editing — modifications to embryos that would be heritable by future generations. This is currently illegal in most jurisdictions, following the widely condemned case of Chinese researcher He Jiankui, who in 2018 created the first gene-edited human babies without adequate oversight or consent. The scientific consensus is that germline editing requires considerably more development and a much more robust ethical framework before any clinical application is appropriate.

title: The Mathematics of Everyday Life: Hidden Patterns in the Ordinary World
body:
Mathematics is often presented as a specialised technical discipline, relevant to scientists and engineers but peripheral to everyday life. This is a significant misrepresentation. Mathematical structures are woven through the ordinary world in ways that, once noticed, are impossible to unsee.

The Fibonacci sequence — in which each number is the sum of the two preceding ones (1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21...) — appears in the arrangement of leaves around a stem, the spiral of a nautilus shell, the pattern of sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees. This is not coincidence. These arrangements minimise overlap and maximise exposure to light and resources through a mathematical optimisation that evolution has independently discovered countless times.

The normal distribution — the bell curve — describes the distribution of an extraordinary range of natural and social phenomena: height, blood pressure, measurement error, exam scores, the distance of shots from a target. The central limit theorem explains why: the sum of many independent random variables, regardless of their individual distributions, tends toward the normal distribution. This mathematical fact underlies most of classical statistics and a significant portion of modern medicine, economics, and social science.

Network theory — the mathematics of connections — governs the behaviour of everything from social media to electrical grids to the spread of infectious disease. The discovery of scale-free networks (in which a small number of nodes have far more connections than the vast majority) has transformed our understanding of how complex systems behave and fail.

These are not esoteric discoveries. They are the hidden architecture of the world — available to anyone who learns to look.

title: Artificial Intelligence: What It Actually Is and What It Actually Does
body:
Few technologies have been more consistently misrepresented — by both advocates and critics — than artificial intelligence. The misrepresentations tend in two directions: the hype that presents AI as approaching or exceeding general human intelligence, and the fear that presents it as an existential threat in the near term. Both misrepresentations obscure what is actually remarkable and genuinely important about current AI systems.

Contemporary AI — specifically, the large language models that have driven the recent wave of public attention — are, at their core, extraordinarily sophisticated pattern recognition and completion systems. They are trained on vast quantities of text (and increasingly, other forms of data), developing statistical models of the relationships between words, concepts, and contexts that allow them to generate coherent and often surprising outputs in response to prompts.

What they do well is genuinely impressive: they can synthesise information across domains at a scale no individual human can match, produce coherent first drafts of an enormous range of written material, write and debug code, translate languages, explain complex concepts accessibly, and perform many other tasks that previously required significant human expertise. Their limitations are equally real: they hallucinate (produce plausible but false information) with disconcerting confidence, they have no grounding in the physical world, they cannot verify claims against reality, and they have no genuine understanding of the material they process.

The productive relationship with these tools is neither credulous nor dismissive: using them where they genuinely help, maintaining human oversight where their limitations matter, and resisting the temptation to anthropomorphise what are, ultimately, very sophisticated autocomplete systems.

title: The Deep Ocean: Earth's Last Great Unexplored Frontier
body:
The deep ocean — defined as water below 200 metres — covers approximately 65% of the Earth's surface and represents 95% of the Earth's living space by volume. It is also the least explored environment on Earth: we have more detailed maps of the surface of the Moon than of the ocean floor. More humans have walked on the Moon than have visited the deepest point of the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench.

The reasons for this relative neglect are practical: the deep ocean is extraordinarily hostile to exploration. Pressures at depth are crushing — at the bottom of the Mariana Trench, approximately eleven kilometres below sea level, the pressure is about 1,100 times atmospheric pressure. Temperatures approach freezing. There is no light. Developing vehicles capable of withstanding these conditions, while also being capable of useful scientific work, is a significant engineering challenge.

What exploration has revealed has been consistently surprising. Life in the deep ocean is far more abundant and diverse than early theories predicted. Hydrothermal vents — discovered only in 1977 — support entire ecosystems fuelled not by sunlight but by chemosynthesis: bacteria and archaea that derive energy from the chemical reactions between vent fluids and seawater, forming the base of food chains that include tube worms, crabs, shrimp, and fish. These ecosystems have prompted serious reconsideration of the conditions under which life can exist — with implications for the search for life on other worlds.

The deep ocean also plays a critical role in Earth's climate system, storing vast amounts of heat and carbon dioxide, driving global ocean circulation patterns, and providing services to the planetary system that we have barely begun to understand.

title: Why the Scientific Method Is the Most Powerful Tool Humans Have Ever Invented
body:
Science is often misunderstood as a body of knowledge — a collection of facts about the world. It is more accurately understood as a method: a systematic approach to asking questions about reality that has proven extraordinarily effective at generating reliable knowledge. What distinguishes it from other ways of knowing is not the questions it asks (which overlap considerably with those of philosophy, religion, and common sense) but the constraints it places on what counts as an acceptable answer.

The core constraint is empirical testability. A scientific claim must make predictions about observable phenomena that could, in principle, be found to be false. This is Karl Popper's criterion of falsifiability — not a definition of truth, but a criterion of scientificity. A claim that cannot be tested cannot be shown to be wrong; a claim that cannot be shown to be wrong contributes nothing to our knowledge of the world.

The second major constraint is replication. A scientific finding must be reproducible by independent investigators using the same methods. This constraint has proven surprisingly difficult to meet — the replication crisis of the past decade, in which a significant proportion of published results in psychology, medicine, and other fields failed to replicate, has been one of the most important developments in the philosophy and practice of science. It has not invalidated the scientific method; it has revealed ways in which the practice of science had drifted from its principles and prompted significant methodological reform.

What science cannot do is equally important to understand. It cannot address questions of value, meaning, or purpose — what we ought to do, what makes life worth living, what we owe to each other. These are genuine and important questions. They simply require different methods, and the authority of science does not extend to them.
